methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $180M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $2.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Watershed
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1.8B

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Zanskar, a Series C climate and energy company founded by Carl Hoiland and Joel Edwards, has raised $180M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparables universe of 9 peers (1 public, 8 private), including same-stage companies KoBold Metals ($2.0B), Watershed ($1.8B), Electric Hydrogen ($1.0B), and Amogy ($700M).

Two triangulation methods are applicable. Stage-progression analysis applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C advancement, yielding $306M. Peer-median analysis across the 4 same-sector, same-stage comps produces a median valuation of $1.4B, with a range of $700M to $2.0B. The dispersion across this peer set is material, reflecting heterogeneity in technology maturity and capital efficiency within the climate tech sector.

Synthesizing both methods yields a triangulated range of $260M (low) to $1.6B (high), with a median of $853M. The wide spread reflects tension between the stage-progression method, anchored to capital raised, and the peer-median approach, which captures market pricing of comparable ventures. Key uncertainties include the appropriate peer selection—stage-matched peers vary substantially in valuation—and whether Zanskar's technology, stage, or capital efficiency materially diverges from the comparison set. Near-term fundraising dynamics and market conditions for climate tech will likely narrow this range.
